schools.summary: Ellicott: Ahithophel rightly saw all turned on an immediate night blow against a weary David; he notes the chronological puzzle of 'this night.'
Clarke: had the counsel been followed, only a miraculous interposition of God could have saved David and his little troop.
Henry: 'smite the shepherd and the sheep scatter'; the plot mirrors Caiaphas against the Son of David, yet is turned to foolishness.
Gill anchors the timing of Ahithophel's renewed counsel.
'Make him afraid' is too weak: a sudden night attack throws David's band into panic so the king may be seized; the plan pleased Absalom and the elders.
Gill: Ahithophel would surprise David while weary with travel and grief and seeking sleep, in no posture of defence.
Wesley: that all Israel should join Absalom is amazing, but 'the finger of God was in it' — a chastisement on David.
Clarke: David is the soul of the whole; secure or destroy him and all Israel submits.
Ellicott: with artful flattery Ahithophel assumes Absalom is already rightful king, the people needing only to be 'brought back.'
Gill: he means not to imprison David's men but to reduce all Israel to Absalom's obedience at one stroke.

themes: the king as hinge of the kingdom

schools.summary: Gill: the plan was well contrived, but it is amazing that Absalom, and stranger still that Israel's elders, should approve the murder of so just and merciful a king.
Gill: it is remarkable providence that Absalom, with Ahithophel's counsel universally approved, should want to hear David's known friend Hushai.
Wesley: a wonderful effect of Providence; there is no contending with the God who can arm a man against himself.
Ellicott/K&D: at the surface, prudent Absalom seeks a second famous counsellor; Hushai answers that this time the advice is not good.
Gill: Hushai comes, very probably into the council chamber itself — David's friend now admitted to the rebellion's secret deliberations.
Ellicott: 'not good at this time' concedes Ahithophel's past wisdom, lending Hushai an air of candour; K&D outline his portrait of David's men as a bear robbed of her young.
Gill: given leave to speak, Hushai answers the picture of a weak-handed king with one of valiant men not to be scattered.
Gill: Hushai reverses Ahithophel's estimate — David's men are mighty and valiant, not weak-handed and easily cowed.
Clarke: 'a bear robbed of her whelps' — beasts are furious when bereaved; David's veterans would fight with a bereaved creature's rage.
K&D: David, a man of war, will not lodge with the people, so no sleeping camp lies open to surprise.
Gill: David, long practised in hiding-places since Saul's day, could lie in ambush and fall on Absalom's men unawares.
Wesley: a first small success from ambush would animate David's men and intimidate Absalom's raw army, presaging total defeat.
Gill: the report of one reverse would melt the bravest heart, even Ahithophel's; he expounds the lion as the emblem of courage (labi / leb) that Hushai says will fail.
Ellicott: Hushai magnifies every hazard and proposes a risk-free muster of all Israel (Dan to Beersheba), assuming with flattery that all are Absalom's.
Wesley: the pretence is sure but slow work; the design is to gain David time and cool the people, and Absalom's personal command is bait for his vanity.
Gill opens Hushai's counsel with 'Therefore I counsel.'
Gill: a vast host spread abroad cannot fail to find David, where twelve thousand might miss him.
Wesley: 'as the dew' means plenteously, suddenly, irresistibly, on all sides — a promise of total destruction.
K&D render the figure; Hushai answers Ahithophel's speed and single target with mass and comprehensiveness, none left.
K&D and Ellicott: pure hyperbole of a united Israel's power, dragging a city to the brook, meant to dazzle Absalom's vanity.
Wesley: a hyperbolical expression suited to the vain-glorious young man, implying numbers enough for anything.
Gill supposes the case of David sheltering in a strong fortified city.

debates.question: Is 'bring ropes' literal, or does the word mean something else?

sides.position: hyperbole with the plain word 'ropes' (chabalim), to display irresistible force
the Chaldee reads 'towers' (mashreyan), since no city is pulled down with ropes

themes: dragging the city to the river

schools.summary: K&D: Hushai's arguments were sound; the deception was assuming all Israel would rally to Absalom, a blindness in which the divine sentence took effect.
Henry: much as man's policy can do, God's providence does more, blinding Absalom's mind; the former counsel followed to correct David, the latter defeated to spare him.
Wesley: the Lord turns all hearts as rivers of water and laughs at men's projects against his children.
Gill: the very elders who applauded Ahithophel now reverse themselves — the text lays the reversal to God's hand.

debates.question: Why did Absalom prefer the worse counsel?

sides.position: human craft — Hushai's flattering, boastful plan pleased the vain young men
divine sovereignty — the LORD appointed to defeat Ahithophel and blinded Absalom's mind

schools.summary: K&D: Hushai sends both counsels to David by the priests, urging him to cross Jordan at once lest he be swallowed up.
Henry: providence guards David by humble means — priests slipping out by En-rogel and a 'poor simple' girl made a trusty messenger.
Gill: Hushai went straight from the council to Zadok and Abiathar, his appointed channel to David.
Ellicott: a council that swung once could swing back, so David must cross Jordan at once; K&D place the couriers at the Rogel spring.
Wesley: the fear is lest minds change and Ahithophel yet persuade a swift pursuit.
Gill: Hushai bids them send quickly and tell David what was advised on both sides.
Ellicott/K&D: En-rogel a border fountain (two proposed sites); the priests' known loyalty made the city unsafe for their sons, who used the water-drawing as cover.
Clarke: En-rogel = 'fullers' well' (ein + regel); 'wench,' unique in the English Bible, simply means maidservant, perhaps restored by King James; Wesley notes the girl's pretence of drawing water.
Gill: the Targum's 'fuller's fountain'; Rogel from the treading of feet.

sides.position: the Fountain of the Virgin or Job's Well, on the Benjamin-Judah border
the Rogel spring at the south-east corner of Jerusalem

outliers.note: A long philological digression on 'wench' — its unique occurrence, Anglo-Saxon/Gothic roots, and probable restoration by King James.

schools.summary: Gill, citing Josephus: a lad (horsemen) saw the couriers near Jerusalem and told Absalom.
K&D: Absalom had likely set spies; noticed, the two hid in a dry cistern in a courtyard at Bahurim on the road to Jordan.
Gill: the householder's wife spread a cloth over the well's mouth so no well could be seen.
K&D and Ellicott: she scattered 'ripoth' (peeled grain, as in Proverbs 27:22) to dry, so nothing was noticed; the Vulgate, 'as if drying peeled barley.'
Wesley: the corn spread to dry shows it was summer-time.
K&D and Ellicott: the rare 'little water-brook' (mikal) misdirects the pursuers; Ellicott sets the deceit beside Rahab and Michal, noting the historian records it without comment.
Wesley: 'over the brook of water' meant over Jordan — 'a manifest lie.'
Gill: the servants, sent off on a false trail, returned empty to Jerusalem.

debates.question: How should the woman's deception be judged?

sides.position: recorded without comment, to be weighed like Rahab's and Michal's
a manifest lie, not to be justified though the outcome was good

schools.summary: K&D: the couriers rise from the well and warn David to cross; by morning not one man was missed who had not gone over Jordan.
Gill: after the servants departed, the warning David had prayed for reached him in time to cross.
K&D: not one was missing at the crossing; the Jordan now bars the pursuers and Mahanaim becomes a rallying-point.
Henry: 'there lacked not one' makes David a type of Messiah, who lost none of those given him (John 18:9).
Gill: David crossed the river in taking Hushai's advice.
K&D and Ellicott: Ahithophel shrewdly foresaw Absalom's ruin and his own traitor's death; David's prayer was fulfilled.
Clarke: no lunacy but deliberate, expecting no mercy; Wesley: contempt on man's wisdom, honour to God's justice — the wicked snared in his own work.
Henry: pride (wisdom must die with him) and fear (a criminal before David's justice) drove the deliberate suicide of 'the brother of a fool.'
Gill: Ahithophel acted when he saw Hushai's counsel, not his, was followed.

debates.question: What chiefly drove Ahithophel to suicide?

sides.position: shrewd foresight of Absalom's certain ruin and his own traitor's death
wounded pride that another's counsel was preferred, joined to fear of David's justice

schools.summary: Gill: Mahanaim, in Gad beyond Jordan, where the angels met Jacob (Genesis 32:2) and Ishbosheth once reigned.
K&D and Ellicott: a fortified city near the Jabbok ford, its old fitness as Ish-bosheth's capital now making it David's refuge and rallying-point.
Wesley: Absalom crossed not speedily but only when all Israel had gathered per Hushai's counsel — the delay that spares David.

K&D and Ellicott: Amasa replaces Joab; 'Israelite' is likely an error for 'Ishmaelite' (per 1 Chronicles 2:17); Abigail's parentage (sister of Zeruiah, yet in 1 Chronicles 2:16 of Jesse's sons) is left uncertain.
Clarke: Joab and Amasa were sisters' children, both nephews to David.
Gill: Amasa was set over the host in Joab's room, Joab being with David.

debates.question: Was Amasa's father an 'Israelite' or an 'Ishmaelite'?

sides.position: 'Ishmaelite,' the received 'Israelite' being a copyist's error (cf. 1 Chronicles 2:17)
the text stands; Amasa and Joab are cousins, nephews to David

schools.summary: Gill: Israel and Absalom pitched in Gilead (Reuben, Gad, half-Manasseh) to give David battle.
Ellicott maps Gilead and notes Absalom never reached Mahanaim before defeat; K&D mark the first sign of David's remaining support in the provisions sent.
Ellicott/K&D: Shobi (perhaps Hanun's brother left as governor), Machir who had kept Mephibosheth (2 Samuel 9:4), and Barzillai supply David — the first proof he was not deserted.
Wesley: Shobi had disowned the insult to David's ambassadors and was left to govern Ammon's remnant; kindness shown is now repaid.
Henry: do good to all men while you may, 'for he that watereth shall be watered' (Proverbs 11:25); God supplies from strangers what families deny.
Gill: beds for men who fled without any; the LXX reckoned ten, of double-woven tapestry.
Clarke: beds were skins and mats, basins wooden bowls, vessels sun-baked clay; parched grain like the Moors' journey-provision carried in a leathern 'bottle.'
K&D list the whole provision; the catalogue measures a real deliverance for a company that crossed Jordan empty-handed.
Gill: honey and butter, much in use among the ancients and the food of Judea, were strength for a company newly out of the wilderness.
Ellicott: 'cheese of kine' is a word found only here and uncertain — English follows the Chaldee/Syriac, the Vulgate 'fat calves,' Theodotion 'sucking calves.'
Wesley: because they were hungry and weary in the wilderness, God 'makes up from strangers the comfort denied in their own families.'
